one_liner: Heineken backs the bars that built it.
search_doc:
Heineken backs the bars that built it.
Mechanisms: long-term-brand-platform, channel-as-customer, category-disruption-rebrand, purpose-driven-B2B, sales-enablement-as-creative
Idea: Bars exist as much as life needs bars. That's why, 4 years ago, Heineken's long-term commitment to back the bars began. Since then, the brand carried out many different activities to help bars overcome whatever business and social challenge they faced, to keep them going and well alive.
Insight: Bars are Heineken's distribution channel, brand engine and emotional home — when bars suffer, the brand suffers, so investing in bars' survival is the most strategic B2B move the brewer can make.

extracted.tagline: A long-term commitment from Heineken to keep bars alive.
extracted.context: The pandemic, the great resignation, energy crisis, inflation. These are just some of the challenges that bars have faced over the last 4 years because everytime the world struggles, bars struggle. And since bars have always been Heineken's biggest supporters, now it's time for Heineken to back the bars.
extracted.idea: Bars exist as much as life needs bars. That's why, 4 years ago, Heineken's long-term commitment to back the bars began. Since then, the brand carried out many different activities to help bars overcome whatever business and social challenge they faced, to keep them going and well alive.
execution: 2020 - Ads campaigns supporting reopening bars
2022 - Boycott Ads, agreeing not to advertise inside closed bars
2023 - Beer Sponsor activation reframing competitor sponsorship of bar venues
2023 - Beer Experience activation transferring historic pub interiors as branded experiences
2024 - Pub Museums, turning closed pubs into protected museums

inferred.insight: Bars are Heineken's distribution channel, brand engine and emotional home — when bars suffer, the brand suffers, so investing in bars' survival is the most strategic B2B move the brewer can make.
inferred.one_liner: Heineken backs the bars that built it.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Four-year platform turning Heineken's biggest B2B customer — the bar — into the hero of its global brand strategy; drove +13.9% bar trust, +13.3% Heineken stocking and +6.95% sell-out volume across 5,500 bars.

visual.style_description: Hero shot of a historic green pub façade ('Mother Mac's') with the Heineken red star sign mounted above the door and the script tagline 'Backing the Bars' overlaid; press logo strip running across the very top, four labelled execution-photo blocks in a timeline along the middle, big metric numbers across the bottom band.

idea.rationale: The 'Backing the Bars' platform reframes Heineken's marketing budget into a long-term commitment to its on-trade B2B customers (bars) rather than to consumer storytelling. It is a coherent platform but functions as a retrospective umbrella for several previously-released ideas (Boycott Ads, Beer Sponsor, Beer Experience, Pub Museums) rather than a single net-new creative idea.

strategy.rationale: Heineken is the Dutch global brewer whose largest B2B distribution channel is the on-trade — bars, pubs and HoReCa venues that pour and pull its beer. The platform is exact mission-fit because the bar IS Heineken's customer in B2B terms, and defending the channel defends the brand. The context (pandemic, energy crisis, inflation pressures on bars) is plausible but unevidenced with channel-closure data, and the four-year period stitches multiple campaigns together without a single sharp insight anchoring the whole platform.

execution.rationale: The four-year timeline strings together five activations — reopening ads (2020), Boycott Ads (2022), Beer Sponsor and Beer Experience (2023), Pub Museums (2024) — across paid, earned and policy channels. The press footprint is broad (BBC, CNN, Wired, The Irish Times, La Repubblica), which fits a long-running B2B brand platform, but the board functions more as a portfolio overview than a single-campaign orchestration.

impact.rationale: '+13.3% bars stocked Heineken in 2023' and '+6.95% sell-out volume increase' are the right B2B trade-channel outcomes (distribution and pull-through), and 'EUR 6.8M invested across 5,500 bars' anchors the platform with absolute scale. However, the percentages are stated without an absolute base (13.3% of which bar universe?) and the platform combines four years of separate campaigns, making causal attribution to any single execution weak. The '+13.9% bars' trust in Heineken' is the kind of relationship metric a B2B jury values but is unsourced on the board.

why_it_won.verdict: Bronze because the platform correctly recognises the bar as Heineken's primary B2B customer, but the case reads as a four-year portfolio recap rather than a single sharp idea, and the channel-level metrics lack absolute bases.

one_liner: Ketchup imposters caught; only Heinz earns the bottle.
search_doc:
Ketchup imposters caught; only Heinz earns the bottle.
Mechanisms: social-listening-as-strategy, brand-truth-reaffirmation, consumer-pain-as-advocacy, category-fraud-exposure
Idea: To drive conversation and awareness, we launched a campaign to expose Ketchup Fraud to the world, using this true pain point to trigger an emotional response in our fans, while turning their voice into ours, getting them to share the campaign and demand restaurants serve Heinz for us. With such a true, universal insight, we were able to earn global coverage.
Insight: This insight was inspired by a real social behaviour found everywhere from reddit to snapchat. Through social listening, we found proof that customers were filling Heinz Ketchup bottles with off-brand ketchup at our fans were noticing and posting about it. This social conversation allowed us to subvert a real consumer pain point to reaffirm our brand truth: It Has To Be Heinz.

extracted.tagline: Heinz exposes ketchup imposters, turning ketchup fans into our voice.
extracted.insight: This insight was inspired by a real social behaviour found everywhere from reddit to snapchat. Through social listening, we found proof that customers were filling Heinz Ketchup bottles with off-brand ketchup at our fans were noticing and posting about it. This social conversation allowed us to subvert a real consumer pain point to reaffirm our brand truth: It Has To Be Heinz.
extracted.idea: To drive conversation and awareness, we launched a campaign to expose Ketchup Fraud to the world, using this true pain point to trigger an emotional response in our fans, while turning their voice into ours, getting them to share the campaign and demand restaurants serve Heinz for us. With such a true, universal insight, we were able to earn global coverage.
execution: Hidden-camera films of customers refilling Heinz bottles with off-brand ketchup at diners
Press ads with declarations: 'Even when it isn't Heinz, it has to be Heinz'
Out-of-home posters
Taxi-top placements
On-site activations at restaurants

inferred.context: Restaurants and diners commonly refill empty Heinz bottles with cheaper off-brand ketchup, a practice fans publicly complained about online.
inferred.one_liner: Ketchup imposters caught; only Heinz earns the bottle.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Turned a viral consumer complaint into a B2B accountability campaign that earned coverage in 45 countries, a 128x engagement benchmark and an 8% year-on-year sales lift.

visual.style_description: Deep red brand-coloured board with the 'Ketchup Fraud' wordmark stacked centrally under the Heinz crest; three hidden-camera kitchen stills sit in a horizontal strip showing staff topping off Heinz bottles with off-brand ketchup, flanked by social-quote tags; two text blocks (Insight / Idea) sit below over the red background, with a thin metric strip and small OOH/taxi thumbnails at the bottom.

idea.rationale: Exposing restaurants that refill Heinz bottles with cheaper off-brand ketchup as 'ketchup fraud' is a sharp consumer-truth that doubles as B2B pressure on foodservice operators. The hidden-camera framing weaponises consumer outrage into a procurement argument: serve real Heinz or get publicly called out.

strategy.rationale: Heinz is Kraft Heinz's flagship condiments brand whose B2B business depends on foodservice operators (diners, restaurants, QSR) ordering genuine Heinz rather than substituting cheaper unbranded ketchup at the pour. The idea is on-strategy for the 'It Has To Be Heinz' platform, but the B2B fit is partial — the campaign primarily addresses consumers and uses them as proxy advocates rather than speaking directly to procurement or restaurant operators with B2B channels (sales enablement, trade press, foodservice distributors). The insight (refilling fraud surfaced via social listening) is grounded in observable Reddit and Snapchat behaviour, which is credible context.

execution.rationale: The hidden-camera films, declarative press ads ('Even when it isn't Heinz, it has to be Heinz'), OOH, taxi-tops and on-site restaurant activations are all consumer-facing channels that pressure foodservice operators indirectly. The execution is competent but leans on classic consumer paid-media rather than B2B-specific channels (LinkedIn, foodservice trade press, distributor sell-in tools), which is a native-to-category soft spot.

impact.rationale: '+8% sales vs the previous year' is the right named business outcome and is the strongest signal in the case, though it is unanchored from an absolute base (8% of which channel — retail, foodservice, total?) and not separated into B2B foodservice contracts won. '128x engagement rate benchmark' and '92% positive engagement' are percentages without an absolute base and entrant-reported, and '45 countries covered' treats reach as a claim rather than audited press. No restaurant-operator switching, foodservice-contract or distributor-volume metric is disclosed, which is the B2B outcome the category demands.

why_it_won.verdict: Bronze because the social-listening insight is genuinely sharp and the +8% sales lift is anchored, but the campaign uses consumers as proxy pressure rather than addressing the foodservice B2B audience directly through native B2B channels.

one_liner: The MRI noise becomes a children's audiobook.
search_doc:
The MRI noise becomes a children's audiobook.
Mechanisms: product-feature-as-content, sales-enablement-as-creative, everyday-object-transformation, B2B-product-storytelling
Idea: Siemens, a top MRI manufacturer, partnered with children's book authors to create a unique library of audiobooks composed from real MRI scan sounds. Each Magnetic Story is customised to the sounds of the most common pediatric MRI scans, turning their dreadful sounds into a delightful narrative.
Insight: MRI scanners scare children, which slows the radiology workflow — if hospitals can transform the noise into part of a story, the scariest part of the device becomes its differentiator at procurement time.

full_record.file_path: 2024/CREATIVE B2B/BRONZE/Magnetic Stories - Siemens Healthinners (Area 23, An Ipg Health Network Company, New York.jpg

extracted.tagline: Transforming a scary medical device into a storytelling tool for children.
extracted.context: MRI sounds can be louder than a military jet. They are scary for children and they present a barrier to care for the radiology team.
extracted.idea: Siemens, a top MRI manufacturer, partnered with children's book authors to create a unique library of audiobooks composed from real MRI scan sounds. Each Magnetic Story is customised to the sounds of the most common pediatric MRI scans, turning their dreadful sounds into a delightful narrative.
execution: Each author wrote a Magnetic Story corresponding to the sounds of one specific MRI scan
The audiobooks play in synch with the scan, with the respective MRI sounds embedded in the narrative
Collaboration with hospital and imaging clinics
Siemens Healthineers incorporated the Magnetic Stories as an introduction feature of its scanners
metrics: Hours of original stories crafted around predictable scan sequences

inferred.insight: MRI scanners scare children, which slows the radiology workflow — if hospitals can transform the noise into part of a story, the scariest part of the device becomes its differentiator at procurement time.
inferred.one_liner: The MRI noise becomes a children's audiobook.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Recast the MRI scanner's terrifying noise as the soundtrack to bespoke children's audiobooks, giving Siemens Healthineers a tangible procurement differentiator for hospitals buying pediatric imaging equipment.

visual.style_description: Dark cinematic hero photograph of a young girl lying inside an MRI scanner wearing headphones, with glowing magenta and cyan energy ribbons flowing from her head; the script logo 'Magnetic Stories' sits over her midsection, three small text columns labelled 'Problem', 'Idea' and 'Execution' below, and a bottom strip of seven coloured audiobook waveform thumbnails.

idea.rationale: Turning the MRI scanner's terrifying noise — louder than a military jet — into the soundtrack of bespoke children's audiobooks synchronised to each scan sequence is an elegant product-feature reframe. The scariest attribute of the device becomes a procurement differentiator for pediatric imaging, which is a sharp B2B angle for a typically-engineering category.

strategy.rationale: Siemens Healthineers is the German medical-imaging giant whose B2B customers are hospital procurement teams choosing MRI vendors against GE Healthcare and Philips. The idea is exact mission-fit because pediatric care is a real procurement criterion for children's hospitals, and a built-in audiobook feature converts a soft-skill problem (scared children, delayed scans) into a hard product differentiator. The context cites the MRI noise level credibly but the board does not anchor the underlying business problem with hospital-workflow or pediatric-cancellation data.

execution.rationale: Each audiobook is composed to map exactly onto the predictable sound sequences of a specific pediatric MRI scan, and Siemens Healthineers incorporated the Magnetic Stories as an introduction feature of its scanners — making the creative output an actual product feature shipped to procurement customers. However, the board names no hospital trial sites, no medical-society endorsement, no children's-book-author roster and no trade-press footprint, so the orchestration around the asset is thin.

impact.rationale: The only metric on the board — 'Hours of original stories crafted around predictable scan sequences' — is a craft output, not a business outcome. There is no procurement-deal, hospital-adoption, scan-completion-rate, pediatric-cancellation-reduction or sales-pipeline figure disclosed, and the press_coverage field is empty. For a B2B medical-equipment campaign whose entire premise is winning hospital procurement, the absence of the named business outcome is conspicuous and is the dominant reason this lands at Bronze rather than higher.

why_it_won.verdict: Bronze because the noise-as-storytelling reframe is a real B2B procurement differentiator and the asset ships as a product feature, but the board names no hospital-adoption or procurement-pipeline outcome to substantiate the case.

one_liner: The granny who became Spain's subway sensation.
search_doc:
The granny who became Spain's subway sensation.
Mechanisms: character-as-brand-asset, ordinary-person-as-celebrity, media-as-product, demo-of-own-channel
Idea: JCDecaux turned an anonymous 100-year-old Spanish granny, Marina Prieto, into the face of a subway out-of-home campaign that re-engaged Spain's daily commuters and proved the cultural power of OOH advertising to potential advertisers.
Insight: Advertisers had lost faith in OOH's cultural power, but a single physical subway character can still spark a national conversation that digital media cannot replicate.

full_record.file_path: 2024/CREATIVE B2B/GRAND PRIX/Meet Marina Prieto - JCDECAUX (David Madrid).jpg

extracted.tagline: The unknown 100-year-old granny who revived Spain's subway ads.
extracted.context: In 2023, subway commuters in Spain encountered Marina Prieto, an unknown 100-year-old lady, in JCDecaux's outdoor subway network, leading to broad social and media impact.
extracted.idea: JCDecaux turned an anonymous 100-year-old Spanish granny, Marina Prieto, into the face of a subway out-of-home campaign that re-engaged Spain's daily commuters and proved the cultural power of OOH advertising to potential advertisers.
execution: Subway OOH placements featuring Marina Prieto across Spain's JCDecaux network
Social media and Instagram content building Marina's persona
Earned press coverage in Spanish national outlets
International campaign extension across 14 countries and 185 new brands

inferred.insight: Advertisers had lost faith in OOH's cultural power, but a single physical subway character can still spark a national conversation that digital media cannot replicate.
inferred.one_liner: The granny who became Spain's subway sensation.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Turned JCDecaux's own out-of-home network into a proof-of-effectiveness demo: a fictional centenarian commuter generated record subway ad bookings, doubled media investment, and reactivated B2B faith in OOH advertising.

visual.style_description: Cinematic photo-led hero shot of a centenarian woman seated in a Madrid subway platform, framed inside a real OOH light box with another commuter beside it; press quotes row at the top, dense metric strip with thumbnail OOH placements along the bottom.

idea.rationale: Casting an anonymous 100-year-old subway commuter as a celebrity built inside JCDecaux's own OOH network turns the media owner's product into the case study. The reframe is exact-fit for the B2B audience: media planners and advertisers are sold OOH effectiveness by watching OOH itself manufacture a national star, not by being told it can.

strategy.rationale: JCDecaux is the world's largest out-of-home media owner, whose B2B mission is to convince advertisers that OOH still drives cultural impact in a digital-first media plan. The idea is exact mission-fit because it sells the medium through the medium, targeting the buy-side decision-makers (media agencies, brand marketers) whose budgets have shifted to digital. The board frames a real B2B problem — eroding advertiser faith in OOH — though the underlying insight is stated as a market truth rather than evidenced with category spend data.

execution.rationale: The orchestration runs subway-station OOH as the launch surface, lets Instagram and earned press build Marina's persona, then converts attention into a B2B sales pitch through international extension across 14 countries and 185 new brands. The choice to anchor the campaign in physical subway light boxes — JCDecaux's own inventory — keeps the medium-as-proof loop tight, and the press tier (El Pais, El Mundo, Antena 3) is the right Spanish national footprint for a domestic media-owner pitch.

impact.rationale: The headline business outcomes for a media owner — record bookings, 185 new brands and a doubling of media investment — are the right pipeline metrics a B2B jury wants to see, and they are named directly rather than as surrogates. The 39,285% Instagram growth is unanchored from an absolute base — a percentage that large almost certainly started from a near-zero follower count and reads as inflation when the base is hidden. The 14-country reach and Spanish national press coverage are entrant-reported but credible given the named outlets.

why_it_won.verdict: Wins the Grand Prix because JCDecaux turned its own subway inventory into the demo that re-sold OOH to advertisers, with record bookings and 185 new brands as direct pipeline proof rather than consumer-impression surrogates.

one_liner: Historic Irish pubs become museums to survive.
search_doc:
Historic Irish pubs become museums to survive.
Mechanisms: legislative-loophole-exploitation, category-redefinition, cultural-heritage-reframing, purpose-driven-product
Idea: The Irish Heritage Council offers protected status, tax support and other financial benefits to places of historical significance that are open to the public, such as museums. So Heineken turned historical Irish pubs into museums, making them eligible for tax reductions and allowing their rich past to protect their future.
Insight: Heritage protection laws designed for museums could be applied to historic pubs, transforming a tax burden into a survival mechanism for cultural institutions.

full_record.file_path: 2024/CREATIVE B2B/GOLD/Pub Museums - Heineken (Lepub Milan _ Publici Dublin).jpg

extracted.tagline: Turning historical pubs into real museums.
extracted.context: Irish pubs have been living in a challenging economic environment; since 2005 a quarter of Irish pubs have shut. Buildings that have served this purpose and their communities for hundreds of years have closed, with their cultural and historical legacy preserved as such. While 157 Irish pubs have closed in the past year, no Irish museum has had the same problem.
extracted.idea: The Irish Heritage Council offers protected status, tax support and other financial benefits to places of historical significance that are open to the public, such as museums. So Heineken turned historical Irish pubs into museums, making them eligible for tax reductions and allowing their rich past to protect their future.
execution: Pubs across Ireland turned into virtual museums via app
App with descriptions, audio guides and event souvenirs
Scan the Pub Museum QR code
Activate the experience in the app
Visit the Pub Museum
Buy beers and souvenirs
Featured historic pubs: Mother Mac's 1797, Duke of York 1772, Sean's Bar AD 900, Toner's Pub 1818

inferred.insight: Heritage protection laws designed for museums could be applied to historic pubs, transforming a tax burden into a survival mechanism for cultural institutions.
inferred.one_liner: Historic Irish pubs become museums to survive.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Reclassifies endangered Irish pubs as cultural museums to unlock a 20% tax cut and heritage protections, generating wide international press coverage across business and trade outlets.

visual.style_description: Dark, moody pub-interior backdrop with a large smartphone mockup at the center showing the Pub Museum app; the Heineken Pub Museums logo and tagline sit upper-left, a four-step user-journey appears upper-right, press logo strip across the top, and four historic-pub thumbnails with dates run along the bottom.

idea.rationale: Reclassifying endangered pubs as museums under the Irish Heritage Council's protected-status framework is a legislative-loophole reframe that delivers a hard, tangible B2B benefit — a 20% tax cut — rather than a marketing favour. The idea operates on the brand's actual commercial channel: the pubs that stock and pour Heineken.

strategy.rationale: Heineken is the Dutch global brewer whose B2B distribution depends on on-trade venues, and Irish pubs are both a cultural anchor and a primary route to market. The idea is exact mission-fit because saving pubs is saving Heineken's own distribution and brand habitat — not borrowed purpose. The context is grounded in a concrete, verifiable data point ('157 Irish pubs have closed in the past year', 'a quarter shut since 2005'), which gives the strategic case real B2B weight.

execution.rationale: Each historic pub (Sean's Bar AD 900, Toner's 1818, Mother Mac's 1797, Duke of York 1772) is turned into a museum via a QR-triggered app with descriptions, audio guides and event souvenirs, so the venue qualifies for heritage protections without ceasing to be a pub. The press orchestration spans business titles (Bloomberg, Financial Times, Forbes-tier outlets) and Irish trade media (Irish Examiner, Newstalk), which is the right buy-side and local-policy footprint.

impact.rationale: The 20% tax cut for participating pubs is a concrete, policy-anchored business outcome that materially helps Heineken's distribution partners — exactly the kind of B2B pipeline metric this category rewards. However, the board does not disclose how many pubs were enrolled, what Heineken's on-trade volume change was, or whether the heritage status was a one-off grant or codified for the wider network. The press logo strip is broad and credible, but coverage is treated as a claim rather than an audited impressions figure.

why_it_won.verdict: Lands Gold because the work converts marketing budget into a structural policy win — a heritage-law reclassification that delivers Heineken's distribution partners a tangible 20% tax cut — rather than yet another consumer storytelling layer.

one_liner: Mastercard data pairs Ukrainian and Polish businesses.
search_doc:
Mastercard data pairs Ukrainian and Polish businesses.
Mechanisms: data-as-matchmaker, B2B-platform-for-coexistence, complementarity-mapping, social-integration-via-commerce
Idea: The Room for Everyone campaign brings together Ukrainian and Polish entrepreneurs getting them to open complementary businesses. To make this a reality we created a digital tool that combines transactions, footfall and business complementarity data to help them find mutually beneficial locations.
Insight: Mastercard data revealed a different story: many businesses are complementary, meaning they thrive better when located near one another. This was an opportunity to bring the Ukrainians and Poles together.

extracted.context: The Russian invasion has displaced 1.55 million refugees in neighboring Poland, increasing the country population by 4%. With 10% newly opened business being Ukrainian, refugees were increasingly seen as competition.
extracted.insight: Mastercard data revealed a different story: many businesses are complementary, meaning they thrive better when located near one another. This was an opportunity to bring the Ukrainians and Poles together.
extracted.idea: The Room for Everyone campaign brings together Ukrainian and Polish entrepreneurs getting them to open complementary businesses. To make this a reality we created a digital tool that combines transactions, footfall and business complementarity data to help them find mutually beneficial locations.
execution: Digital tool combining transactions, footfall and complementarity data
Pairings: pet stores near drug stores, bookstores near jewelers, barbershops near restaurants, grocery stores near hairdressers, bakeries near beauty salons
OOH ads with help-wanted style posters featuring Ukrainian and Polish entrepreneurs
metrics: Up to 40% of new business owners have used our tool
+10% positive attitude towards Ukrainians, reversing negative trend

inferred.one_liner: Mastercard data pairs Ukrainian and Polish businesses.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Reversed a negative attitude trend toward Ukrainian refugees in Poland by reframing them as complementary business partners through a Mastercard data tool, used by up to 40% of new business owners.

visual.style_description: Dark navy editorial layout: large 'room for everyone' headline left with Mastercard's red/yellow pin-shaped logo motif, a strip of OOH help-wanted posters featuring entrepreneur portraits across the top, a city-map illustration below showing pinned complementary-business pairings, and a smartphone mockup of the digital pairing tool on the right.

idea.rationale: Using Mastercard's payment-network data to match Ukrainian and Polish entrepreneurs into complementary, mutually-beneficial business locations is a thoughtful data-as-matchmaker mechanic for a small-business B2B audience. The reframing of refugees-as-competition into refugees-as-business-partners is sharp, though the underlying mechanic (data-driven business pairings) has precedents in retail-location analytics.

strategy.rationale: Mastercard is the global payment-network whose proprietary asset for B2B credibility is the transactional and footfall data flowing through its rails. The idea is exact mission-fit because Mastercard sells SME merchants data-driven decisioning, and the campaign demonstrates that capability with one of its harshest live use-cases. The insight is rooted in a specific Mastercard data finding — that complementary nearby businesses thrive together — and the context is anchored in a verifiable cultural fact ('1.55M refugees, +4% population, 10% of new businesses Ukrainian').

execution.rationale: A digital pairing tool combines transactions, footfall and complementarity data to recommend locations, supported by an OOH help-wanted poster series featuring named Ukrainian and Polish entrepreneurs. The tool itself is the B2B sales-enablement deliverable — a real product placed in front of new business owners — but the board names only two press outlets (Business Insider, Euronews) and shows limited evidence of trade-media penetration into Polish SME networks.

impact.rationale: 'Up to 40% of new business owners have used our tool' is the right B2B adoption metric for a Mastercard SME play, though the 'up to' framing and absent absolute base (40% of how many new owners?) softens the claim. The '+10% positive attitude towards Ukrainians' is a credible attitudinal shift but is a social outcome, not the merchant-volume or transaction-fee outcome a B2B jury would weight most. No pipeline, GMV or partner-acquisition figure is shown.

why_it_won.verdict: Lands Silver because Mastercard turned its own payment data into a working SME tool that addresses a sharp political-economic insight, but the impact case leans on an 'up to 40%' adoption figure and an attitudinal lift rather than hard merchant revenue.

one_liner: The spreadsheet becomes the music video.
search_doc:
The spreadsheet becomes the music video.
Mechanisms: platform-jailbreak, sales-enablement-as-creative, media-as-product, tool-hijack, ABM-creative-at-scale
Idea: Spreadbeats. A music video done before, ever- and ear-opening way to prove the power of combining video and audio in a medium perfectly tailored to its audience. We partnered with Coachella headliner artist Sofi Tukker and coded a four-minute music video into the actual spreadsheet. Spotify is available to use as cells, allowing turning rows and columns into a canvas that sprang to life when the file was opened.
Insight: Media planners live in spreadsheets. Every year, for Spotify's holiday smoothie boots out thousands of spreadsheets with media plan proposals to potential clients. So, to win over media planners, we brought Spotify's video ad capabilities and its ROI superiority to the place where they spend the most time considering ad buys: their trusty spreadsheets.

extracted.tagline: The first music video entirely coded and experienced in Excel.
extracted.context: In 2023, Spotify launched new video ad units. But there's a problem: media planners and advertisers still consider us as just an audio platform. Avid audio-only planners on the buy-side aren't reaching out for proposals; they often request media plans tailored exclusively for audio ads. That's a shame because audio ads combined with video on Spotify are 2.2 times more effective than audio ads alone.
extracted.insight: Media planners live in spreadsheets. Every year, for Spotify's holiday smoothie boots out thousands of spreadsheets with media plan proposals to potential clients. So, to win over media planners, we brought Spotify's video ad capabilities and its ROI superiority to the place where they spend the most time considering ad buys: their trusty spreadsheets.
extracted.idea: Spreadbeats. A music video done before, ever- and ear-opening way to prove the power of combining video and audio in a medium perfectly tailored to its audience. We partnered with Coachella headliner artist Sofi Tukker and coded a four-minute music video into the actual spreadsheet. Spotify is available to use as cells, allowing turning rows and columns into a canvas that sprang to life when the file was opened.
execution: Music video coded entirely inside an Excel spreadsheet
Spreadsheet sent as a media-buyer outreach asset
Sofi Tukker single, 'Spread', engineered for cell-by-cell playback
All within one Excel file of 10MB
Distributed directly via email to media planners
Used as a sales-enablement tool to schedule meetings
metrics: 1 million data points turned into ads

inferred.one_liner: The spreadsheet becomes the music video.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Coded a music video into an Excel file and emailed it directly to media planners, hijacking their daily workspace to demonstrate that Spotify's video ad units belong on every media plan.

visual.style_description: Hero close-up of a laptop screen showing a pixelated music video rendered as coloured spreadsheet cells against a bright green pixel grid background; left column of dark text blocks labelled 'The Challenge', 'The Context', 'The Idea', 'A Trip to the Origins'; bottom strip of small case-thumbnail screens.

idea.rationale: Coding a full music video into the cells of a single Excel file and emailing it to media planners hijacks the exact workspace where ad-buy decisions are made. It is a precise platform-jailbreak that proves Spotify's video-plus-audio capability inside the buyer's own tool, which is a stronger creative answer than any conventional sales deck.

strategy.rationale: Spotify is the world's largest audio streaming service, and its B2B mission with this work is to reposition itself in media planners' minds from an audio-only platform to a video-and-audio platform after the 2023 launch of new video ad units. The idea is exact mission-fit because media planners are the buy-side decision-maker named explicitly. The insight ('planners live in spreadsheets', 'audio+video on Spotify is 2.2x more effective than audio alone') gives the strategic case a concrete, brand-owned data point.

execution.rationale: Sofi Tukker's single 'Spread' is engineered for cell-by-cell playback, the entire file lands at 10MB so it clears corporate email filters, and the asset is distributed directly to media planners as both an outreach piece and a meeting-booking tool. The channel choice — email-attached spreadsheet — is the right ABM vector for the named decision-maker, and the orchestration is tightly contained on that single sales-enablement deliverable.

impact.rationale: The board shows the craft of the asset itself (1 million data points turned into ads, 10MB file size) but does not show a B2B outcome: no meetings booked, pipeline value, share-of-video-buy shift, or revenue from media planners is disclosed, and the press_coverage field is empty. For a sales-enablement campaign whose entire premise is winning over media planners, the absence of the named business outcome (meetings, RFPs, video-ad revenue from converted planners) is conspicuous.

why_it_won.verdict: Lands Silver because the spreadsheet-as-music-video is a genuinely original ABM weapon aimed at exactly the right B2B audience, but the board never names the media-planner pipeline outcome that should have been the entire point.

one_liner: The bootleg logo becomes the official one.
search_doc:
The bootleg logo becomes the official one.
Mechanisms: brand-rights-relinquishment, vernacular-as-asset, community-co-branding, purpose-driven-product
Idea: A global platform that partnered with bodegas to validate their Coke-Creations. It celebrates such as Australia, India, Indonesia, South Africa, Brazil, and Mexico. From single retail shop and their home-grown brand identity to everyone in the local community, this turned an irreverent visual problem into a worldwide tribute to local businesses creating Coca-Cola.
Insight: This is a brand that has always celebrated diversity, inclusion and communities. Instead of trying to standardize the unofficial, we decided to embrace and humbly recognize it.

full_record.file_path: 2024/CREATIVE B2B/GOLD/Thanks For Coke Creating - CocaCola (VML, New York _ VML, Kansas City _ VML, Sao Paulo).jpg

extracted.tagline: How Coca-Cola embraced unauthorized versions of its logo in bodegas worldwide.
extracted.context: The Coca-Cola brand is the result of 130 years of carefully crafted and protected visual identity. Around the world, small local shops, bodegas and businesses paint their own unauthorized versions of the Coca-Cola logo, drinks, and color palette that defy any guidelines.
extracted.insight: This is a brand that has always celebrated diversity, inclusion and communities. Instead of trying to standardize the unofficial, we decided to embrace and humbly recognize it.
extracted.idea: A global platform that partnered with bodegas to validate their Coke-Creations. It celebrates such as Australia, India, Indonesia, South Africa, Brazil, and Mexico. From single retail shop and their home-grown brand identity to everyone in the local community, this turned an irreverent visual problem into a worldwide tribute to local businesses creating Coca-Cola.
execution: OOH and outdoor signage retaining the hand-painted local versions of the Coke logo
Local shop signs and storefront identity systems co-branded with bodega owners
Branded merchandise and trucks featuring the unofficial logo variants
Global film and social rollout celebrating bodega owners
press_coverage: Coke shows that imitation is the best form of flattery
Coca-Cola embraces unauthorized versions of its logo - AdAge
Coke decided to just chill out, and it's a winning move - Yahoo!

inferred.one_liner: The bootleg logo becomes the official one.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Reversed a century of brand-guideline policing by certifying bootleg bodega logos as Coca-Cola's official local identity, earning 95% positive sentiment and 3.7 billion in reach for small retailers.

visual.style_description: Warm portrait of a smiling bodega owner in a blue t-shirt seated in front of a hand-painted Coca-Cola wall, surrounded by a dense collage of bodega storefronts, hand-painted Coke signs and product placements; press quote column on the right, big metric numbers across the bottom band.

idea.rationale: Relinquishing 130 years of brand-guideline orthodoxy to certify bootleg hand-painted bodega versions of the logo as official is a category-disruption move at the deepest level of a brand: its visual identity system. The act reframes Coca-Cola's small-shop B2B partners from copyright liabilities into co-authors, which is a genuinely novel posture for a CPG of this scale.

strategy.rationale: Coca-Cola is the world's largest beverage company, whose distribution model in emerging markets rests on millions of independent bodegas, kioscos and corner shops as its trade-channel partners. The idea is exact mission-fit because retail-shop owners ARE the B2B audience the brand depends on for shelf space and visibility — celebrating them protects the channel. The insight is observational ('shops paint their own versions') rather than data-backed with a count of bodegas or unauthorized signage, which limits the rigour of the strategic case.

execution.rationale: The rollout retains the hand-painted signs as the campaign's hero asset, co-brands storefront identity systems with named bodega owners, applies the vernacular logos to trucks and merchandise, and is amplified through a global film and social layer. Markets are explicitly cited (Australia, India, Indonesia, South Africa, Brazil, Mexico), giving the B2B channel relevance across Coke's bottler-and-retail network rather than as a single-country PR play.

impact.rationale: '3.7 billion reach for local shops' is the closest thing to a B2B partner outcome — small retailers gaining visibility they could not buy — but the figure is unaudited and reach is a softer metric than the named-bodega sales lift or shelf-share gain a B2B jury would prefer. '95% positive brand sentiment' is unanchored from a base ('positive among how many surveyed?'), and no pipeline, bottler-level revenue or trade-partner retention number is shown. Press is limited to three quoted headlines, weaker than the press coverage typical at this tier.

why_it_won.verdict: Lands Gold because the brand traded the most sacred B2B asset it owns — its trademarked logo — for genuine co-ownership with its small-retail channel, though the partner-level commercial outcomes are softer than the cultural ones.

search_doc:
Frozen food at -15 instead of -18.
Mechanisms: industry-coalition-building, convention-challenge, unbranded-advocacy, data-led-B2B-content
Idea: There are many different companies in the frozen food supply chain. DP World knew that to persuade them all to make the move to -15, competitors would have to become partners. So they made the academic research data freely available and formed a neutral, unbranded coalition which the world's largest shipping companies, hauliers, warehouses and food producers were happy to join.
Insight: A single industry-wide convention (-18C frozen storage) had never been questioned despite its massive climate cost; competitors will collaborate only when the proof is neutral and unbranded.

full_record.file_path: 2024/CREATIVE B2B/SILVER/The Move To -15 - Dp World (Edenman London).jpg

extracted.tagline: For the last century we have shipped food at the globally recognised standard of -18 degrees Celsius. Every degree with a significant impact on global warming. But does it have to be that cold?
extracted.context: As the world's temperatures continue to rise, established business practices need to be challenged. DP World, global logistics providers, challenged the convention of shipping food at -18 degrees and commissioned a group of independent academics to find out if this long held practice could be transformed to help a warming planet.
extracted.idea: There are many different companies in the frozen food supply chain. DP World knew that to persuade them all to make the move to -15, competitors would have to become partners. So they made the academic research data freely available and formed a neutral, unbranded coalition which the world's largest shipping companies, hauliers, warehouses and food producers were happy to join.
execution: Independent academic research commissioned and shared openly
Neutral unbranded coalition of shipping companies, hauliers, warehouses and food producers
Coalition launched at COP28
Trade-press and earned-media campaign across logistics and climate outlets
metrics: 60% of the global container shipping industry joined up
+11% increase in brand trust for DP World
17.7M tons carbon saving from a 3 degree change
press_coverage: Bloomberg quote: 'Raising temperature of frozen foods by just three degrees from -18C to -15C can slash carbon emissions'
Ex-Prime Minister of Denmark quote: 'It's powerful to see competitors come together on something momentous, which doesn't require new tech, just collaboration'
Logistik Express quote: 'How a 3-degree change in frozen food storage sparks a carbon emission revolution'

inferred.insight: A single industry-wide convention (-18C frozen storage) had never been questioned despite its massive climate cost; competitors will collaborate only when the proof is neutral and unbranded.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Built an unbranded competitor coalition launched at COP28 that recruited 60% of global container shipping and projects 17.7M tons of annual carbon savings, while lifting DP World brand trust by 11%.

visual.style_description: Cinematic dusk seascape with cargo ships at anchor as the hero image, the campaign title 'The Move to -15' set in white and DP-blue serif over the horizon, three short text columns (Background / Idea / Results) below, a trade-press logo row and three pull-quotes across the top, and a three-metric band along the bottom.

idea.rationale: Challenging a century-old, never-questioned industry convention (-18C frozen-food shipping) with three degrees of warmer storage as the climate lever is a sober, technically-credible B2B idea. The move to build a neutral, unbranded coalition rather than a branded campaign is the sharper creative choice, because it lets competitors join without endorsing a rival logistics provider.

strategy.rationale: DP World is the Dubai-headquartered global ports and logistics operator competing with Maersk, MSC and CMA CGM for container shipping share, whose B2B mission is to position itself as the climate-credible operator for global supply chains. The idea is exact mission-fit because frozen-shipping standards are DP World's actual operational domain, and leading the convention change reframes the brand from commodity logistics to industry thought leader. The insight is anchored in independent academic research — a strong B2B credibility signal — and the carbon-saving figure has a stated methodology (3-degree change → 17.7M tons).

execution.rationale: The orchestration commissioned independent academic research first, kept it openly available so competitors could adopt it without trust friction, then assembled an unbranded coalition launched at COP28 to give the announcement governmental-stage credibility. Trade-press placement spans logistics outlets (Cooling Post, ACW, Logistik Express, Greenfleet) and business titles (Forbes, Bloomberg, Yahoo Finance), which is the right buy-side and policy-maker footprint for the B2B audience.

impact.rationale: '60% of the global container shipping industry joined up' is a strong B2B coalition-adoption metric that directly addresses the campaign's stated objective, and '+11% increase in brand trust for DP World' is the right brand-equity outcome for a logistics challenger. The '17.7M tons carbon saving' is presented as a projected figure derived from the 3-degree change rather than a measured outcome — it's a model output, not an audited result. No pipeline, contract win or shipping-volume share-shift is shown, which is the named business outcome a logistics jury would expect.

why_it_won.verdict: Lands Silver because DP World engineered the rare B2B move of getting 60% of its competitors to follow its lead on a climate convention, though the carbon savings remain modelled rather than measured and no contract-pipeline outcome is disclosed.

search_doc:
Truckers earn points by sleeping.
Mechanisms: behaviour-rewards-program, incentive-inversion, wearable-data-as-currency, B2B-loyalty-platform
Idea: That's why the country's leading truck manufacturer, VW Trucks, and Brazil's largest rewards company, Livelo, decided to team up and create the first-ever program where you can earn points by sleeping — improving road safety and providing truck drivers with extra income.
Insight: Truckers must choose between safe rest and extra income; rewarding sleep itself can resolve the trade-off.

full_record.file_path: 2024/CREATIVE B2B/BRONZE/Trucker Napp - Volkswagen & Livelo (ALMAPBBDO, Sao Paulo).jpg

extracted.tagline: The first rewards program for truckers that gives benefits for sleeping.
extracted.context: Every trucker understands the importance of rest, as well as the need to earn more money. However, those needs often collide. Research shows that 42% of traffic accidents are related to sleep deprivation, and truck drivers rank first in fatal work accidents.
extracted.idea: That's why the country's leading truck manufacturer, VW Trucks, and Brazil's largest rewards company, Livelo, decided to team up and create the first-ever program where you can earn points by sleeping — improving road safety and providing truck drivers with extra income.
execution: Download the app and pair with a smartwatch
Each night's sleep quality earns a rating in Livelo points, redeemable for trips, products, services, or cash
Fleet owners discounts on after-sales services, tune-ups, spare parts, accessories
Mobile app available on Google Play
Wearable smartwatch integration for sleep tracking
Microsite at truckernapp.com.br
metrics: 48 of the top 100 carriers signed up for the program
7.21 hours of sleep per night on average
4 Latin American countries developing their own initiatives

inferred.insight: Truckers must choose between safe rest and extra income; rewarding sleep itself can resolve the trade-off.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Signed 48 of Brazil's top 100 carriers, distributed over 1M Livelo points and drove 550% more leads than average, while sparking copycat sleep-rewards initiatives in four Latin American countries.

visual.style_description: Cinematic close-up of a truck driver's smiling face dominates the left two-thirds with the campaign title 'TRUCKER NAPP' set in spaced white capitals across it, a moon/sleep icon swapped in mid-word; a vertical strip of seven app-screen and on-road thumbnails runs down the right edge; four short text blocks (problem / idea / download / sleep=earnings / fleet discounts) and a metric band sit across the bottom.

idea.rationale: Paying truckers to sleep — converting rest into Livelo loyalty points via a smartwatch — is an incentive-inversion that resolves the named trade-off between safety and income. Pairing a truck manufacturer with a rewards-program company to deliver that mechanic is a credibly co-branded B2B platform aimed at fleet operators and individual drivers.

strategy.rationale: VW Trucks is the truck-manufacturing arm whose B2B customers are Brazilian fleet operators, and Livelo is Brazil's largest rewards program — the partnership targets fleet purchase decision-makers via the driver retention pain point. The idea is on-strategy for VW's brand-safety positioning in the heavy-vehicle category. The context is anchored in a concrete, verifiable safety statistic ('42% of traffic accidents related to sleep deprivation, truck drivers rank first in fatal work accidents'), which gives the B2B case real procurement weight.

execution.rationale: The orchestration combines an app paired with a smartwatch for sleep tracking, a points-earning mechanic redeemable for trips, products, services or cash, after-sales discounts for fleet owners, and a microsite at truckernapp.com.br. Distribution is via Google Play plus Brazilian trucker-trade press (Blog do Caminhoneiro, Frota News, Transporte, Estradão), which is the right vertical-press footprint to reach the named fleet-owner audience.

impact.rationale: '48 of the top 100 carriers signed up' is the strongest B2B outcome — an anchored adoption number within a named universe — and is the right pipeline metric for a fleet-targeted VW play. '550% more leads than average' is unanchored from an absolute base (550% of which baseline?) and reads as inflated when the base is hidden. '7.21 hours of sleep per night on average' is a behavioural surrogate, not a fleet-revenue or truck-sale outcome, and the '1M Livelo points distributed' is a program-throughput figure, not a VW commercial result. The Brazilian trade-press coverage is credible but limited to local outlets.

why_it_won.verdict: Bronze because the sleep-as-points mechanic is genuinely original and 48 of Brazil's top 100 carriers signed up — a concrete B2B adoption anchor — but the headline percentages are unbased and no VW truck-sales outcome is disclosed.
